pages.text: One day, Al the raccoon strolled through the forest searching for a tasty treat. He stumbled upon a locked box. “Cool, that’s fun,” said Al. “I wonder if there’s any yummy food inside?”
As he crept closer, he spotted a scribbled note on the box. It read: “To open this box, use the Al Go Rithm.”

“That’s fun,” thought Al. But he had never heard of his own go rhythm. He remembered dancing a silly jig once when he found an old hot dog in a dumpster—maybe that was it? He tried the little dance again, but the box wouldn’t budge.
As his stomach growled, Al sighed and resumed his trek through the woods. Now, he wasn’t just hungry—he was also stuck wondering the biggest question of all: What was Al’s Go Rhythm?

As he wandered on, he met his old friend the squirrel. “Hey, Sam,” Al asked, “do you know what my go rhythm might be?”

“Your go rhythm?” Sam replied, flicking his tail. “I’m not sure about yours, but my rhythm shows up when I’m drumming.”
Al figured it was worth a try, so they grabbed Sam’s drums and pounded away every which way. They had a blast exploring all sorts of beats. But no matter what they tried, that box never opened.

Next, Al spotted the friendly deer. “Hey, do you know how I can find my Go Rhythm?” Al asked.

“Go Rhythm?” the deer repeated, tilting its head in confusion. “I’m not sure about that, but my beat-boxing has some serious groove!”

Al figured beatboxing was worth a try, too. So he and the deer started laying down some drippy mouth tracks.

“Buh-tss-kah-tss-buh-tss-kah-tss!”
“Puh-tuh-tuh-tss-chk-chk-pah!”

They freestyled fire for a good while, but no matter how smooth their mouth drumming got, the box stayed shut.

Al then found his old friend, the Skunk. “Hey, Sharla,” Al called, “do you know how I can find my rhythm?”

Sharla flicked her tail gracefully and smiled. “I’m not sure how you’ll find yours, Al,” she replied, “but trust me—there’s plenty of groove when I dance.”

Al was out of ideas, so he and Sharla danced the night away, trying everything—from tap to hip-hop, breakdancing to Kathak.

But no matter how they grooved, spun, or moved, that box stayed shut.

Hungry as ever, Al trudged home. The wise old owl spotted his drooping tail and asked, “Al, what’s the matter?”

“Oh, Owl,” Al sighed, “I found this box that says I can open it if I find my rhythm. But I’ve tried everything, and it still won’t budge.”
The owl fluttered down and studied the note on the box. “Hmm… I believe you’ve misunderstood, Al. It’s not your go rhythm—it’s an al-gor-ithm.”

“An Al-gor-ithm is a set of steps,” Owl explained. “Right below that big word are the instructions you need to open the box.”

Al grabbed the note and realized he’d been so hung up on the word “algorithm” that he forgot to keep reading.

Then Al read the rest of the note:

“Step 1: Find the key beneath the old log.
Step 2: Insert the key into the lock and twist.
Step 3: Lift the lid and enjoy your food.”

Al followed the instructions exactly and —CLICK!—the box popped open. Inside was a feast of tasty treats: ripe berries, crunchy cookies, and old hot dogs! “It worked!” Al exclaimed. “So that’s an Al-Go-Rithm!”
Al invited Sam, the deer, Sharla, and all their other forest friends to a grand feast. They shared tales of their drum duets, beatboxing until their cheeks ached, and dancing under the moon.

As Al looked back on all the fun he’d had, he realized something amazing: an algorithm is just like following a drum pattern, beat boxing out a rhythm, or learning a dance routine! Life is full of adventures, and they all start with the right steps.

The End
